title: Extreme Environments: vocabulary; Extreme Weather around the World
description: 3rd grade vocabulary practice using informational text
topic_path: Extreme Environments: vocabulary; Extreme Weather around the World

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage, then answer the practice question. 

___

##Extreme Weather around the World##

1. Have you ever heard someone complain about the weather? It’s always either too hot, too cold, too wet, too dry, and so on. It seems that we only get a few days a year when the weather is “just right”. But really, all of these complaints seem silly when thinking about the world’s extreme climates. 

![A map of the world highlighting the location of the Atacama Desert in Chile, Mawsynram in India, Dallol in Ethiopia, and Oymyakon in Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/24303be9f6f7c4ca6f16909cb58fb379.jpg)   
*Map of the world*

###Extremely Hot###   

2. Is the harsh, scorching heat making your sandals sweaty? Well, in Dallol, Ethiopia, your shoes could melt if you stand still for a few minutes. Dallol is officially the hottest inhabited place on Earth. It’s part of a desert region that is below sea level. In addition, Dallol sits on top of a volcano. The intense heat [[☃ definition 1]] most of the water that flows into the area, leaving large salt slabs behind. The Afar people of Dallol harvest this salt to sell at local markets.

![The salt slabs of Dallol, Ethiopia, which are green and red in color.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e249d358ad54a6901660db7499a42f3.png)
*Picture 1*

###Extremely Cold###  

3. You don’t know cold unless you’ve been to Oymyakon, Russia. This village is located far to the north in an area called the Sakha Republic. It has the honor of being one of the coldest places in the world where people live year-round. Yet, the word “cold” barely describes it. Try “freeze your eyeballs frigid”. Oymyakon’s temperatures dip far below freezing. Its dwellers have to wear goggles to protect their eyes from very strong winds and severely cold temperatures. The land is mostly inhospitable to farming crops. So the locals eat mainly meat and canned foods brought in from other towns. 

![A snowy street with brightly colored buildings in the small town of Oymyakon, Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6afbe8647f2a675b2a36b2131492d8f6.jpg)
*Picture 2*

###Extremely Dry###

4. Make sure to hug your umbrella the next time you see it. Some areas of Chile’s Atacama desert in South America haven’t seen rain in 400 years. People get water for crops and for drinking by “harvesting” clouds, instead of depending on precipitation. When the fog rolls in from the Pacific Ocean, they collect moisture with plastic sheets that drip into pans underneath.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5532f15ea7cd7f0de3cbe68c219a654e.jpg)
*Picture 3*

###Extremely Wet###

5. Your picnic got rained out? Absorb this: in Mawsynram, India, it basically *always* rains. The jungle village gets enough yearly rain to cover a three-story building. To cope with the steady rainfall, villagers have come up with some clever solutions. They use grass to soundproof their roofs from the constant noise of heavy rain. Outdoor workers rely on traditional *knups* to keep them dry. Woven with bamboo, grasses, and banana leaves, and worn over the head and back, these __coverings__ look like turtle shells. 

6. No matter what your personal weather peeve is, there’s always a way to make the best of it. As the saying goes, “there’s no such thing as bad weather, only the wrong clothes.” People who live in the world's most intense environments realize that you’ve got to adapt to the climate you’ve got. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice Question##

Read the sentence from the passage. 

“Woven with bamboo, grasses, and banana leaves, and worn over the head and back, these __coverings__ look like turtle shells.”

**Based on the root word “cover”, what does __coverings__ mean?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: when a liquid turns to vapor or gas

options.explanation:
Estimated 900L

*Image source: [“Piedras Rojas, Atacama Desert, Chile”](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Piedras_Rojas,_Atacama_Desert,_Chile.jpg) by Wescottm is licensed under [CC BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/deed.en).*

*Image source: [“Oymyakon - 190228 DSC 5642”](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Oymyakon_-_190228_DSC_5642.jpg) by Ilya Varlamov is licensed under [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/deed.en).*

*Image source: [“The hydrothermal system of Dallol, Ethiopia”](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_hydrothermal_system_of_Dallol,_Ethiopia.png) by Electra Kotopoulou is licensed under [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/deed.en).*

choices.content: a thing used to protect something else
a way to build cheap shelters
the act of weaving with natural fabrics
a copy of a popular song

##Extreme Weather around the World##

1. Have you ever heard someone complain about the weather? It’s always either too hot, too cold, too wet, too dry, and so on. It seems that we only get a few days a year when the weather is “just right”. But really, all of these complaints seem silly when thinking about the world’s extreme climates. 

![A map of the world highlighting the location of the Atacama Desert in Chile, Mawsynram in India, Dallol in Ethiopia, and Oymyakon in Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/24303be9f6f7c4ca6f16909cb58fb379.jpg)   
*Map of the world*

###Extremely Hot###   

2. Is the harsh, scorching heat making your sandals sweaty? Well, in Dallol, Ethiopia, your shoes could melt if you stand still for a few minutes. Dallol is officially the hottest inhabited place on Earth. It’s part of a desert region that is below sea level. In addition, Dallol sits on top of a volcano. The intense heat [[☃ definition 1]] most of the water that flows into the area, leaving large salt slabs behind. The Afar people of Dallol harvest this salt to sell at local markets.

![The salt slabs of Dallol, Ethiopia, which are green and red in color.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e249d358ad54a6901660db7499a42f3.png)
*Picture 1*

###Extremely Cold###  

3. You don’t know cold unless you’ve been to Oymyakon, Russia. This village is located far to the north in an area called the Sakha Republic. It has the honor of being one of the coldest places in the world where people live year-round. Yet, the word “cold” barely describes it. Try “freeze your eyeballs frigid”. Oymyakon’s temperatures dip far below freezing. Its dwellers have to wear goggles to protect their eyes from very strong winds and severely cold temperatures. The land is mostly __inhospitable__ to farming crops. So the locals eat mainly meat and canned foods brought in from other towns. 

![A snowy street with brightly colored buildings in the small town of Oymyakon, Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6afbe8647f2a675b2a36b2131492d8f6.jpg)
*Picture 2*

###Extremely Dry###

4. Make sure to hug your umbrella the next time you see it. Some areas of Chile’s Atacama desert in South America haven’t seen rain in 400 years. People get water for crops and for drinking by “harvesting” clouds, instead of depending on precipitation. When the fog rolls in from the Pacific Ocean, they collect moisture with plastic sheets that drip into pans underneath.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5532f15ea7cd7f0de3cbe68c219a654e.jpg)
*Picture 3*

###Extremely Wet###

5. Your picnic got rained out? Absorb this: in Mawsynram, India, it basically *always* rains. The jungle village gets enough yearly rain to cover a three-story building. To cope with the steady rainfall, villagers have come up with some clever solutions. They use grass to soundproof their roofs from the constant noise of heavy rain. Outdoor workers rely on traditional *knups* to keep them dry. Woven with bamboo, grasses, and banana leaves, and worn over the head and back, these coverings look like turtle shells. 

6. No matter what your personal weather peeve is, there’s always a way to make the best of it. As the saying goes, “there’s no such thing as bad weather, only the wrong clothes.” People who live in the world's most intense environments realize that you’ve got to adapt to the climate you’ve got. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

##Practice Question##

Read the sentence from paragraph 6.

“People who live in the world's most __intense__ environments realize that you’ve got to adapt to the climate you’ve got.”

Read this dictionary entry.

**intense** (*adjective*)   
1. of extreme force or strength   
2. showing strong feelings or opinions   
3. highly concentrated, as in a flavor   
4. extremely serious about   

**Which dictionary meaning most closely matches how the word __intense__ is used in the sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

##Practice Question##

Read the sentence from paragraph 2.

“It’s part of a desert __region__ that is below sea level.”

Read the dictionary entry.

**region** (*noun*)   
1. an area of activity or interest   
2. an area surrounding a specific body part   
3. an area of the world that shares similar features   
4. an area that has no boundaries   

**Which dictionary definition best matches the use of the word __region__ in the passage?**

2. most advanced

**Which meaning most closely matches the word __extreme__ as it’s used in the sentence?**

choices.content: at the farthest possible point from the center

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage, then answer the TWO practice questions. 

___

##Extreme Weather around the World##

1. Have you ever heard someone complain about the weather? It’s always either too hot, too cold, too wet, too dry, and so on. It seems that we only get a few days a year when the weather is “just right”. But really, all of these complaints seem silly when thinking about the world’s extreme climates. 

![A map of the world highlighting the location of the Atacama Desert in Chile, Mawsynram in India, Dallol in Ethiopia, and Oymyakon in Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/24303be9f6f7c4ca6f16909cb58fb379.jpg)   
*Map of the world*

###Extremely Hot###   

2. Is the harsh, scorching heat making your sandals sweaty? Well, in Dallol, Ethiopia, your shoes could melt if you stand still for a few minutes. Dallol is officially the hottest inhabited place on Earth. It’s part of a desert region that is below sea level. In addition, Dallol sits on top of a volcano. The intense heat [[☃ definition 1]] most of the water that flows into the area, leaving large salt slabs behind. The Afar people of Dallol harvest this salt to sell at local markets.

![The salt slabs of Dallol, Ethiopia, which are green and red in color.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e249d358ad54a6901660db7499a42f3.png)
*Picture 1*

###Extremely Cold###  

3. You don’t know cold unless you’ve been to Oymyakon, Russia. This village is located far to the north in an area called the Sakha Republic. It has the honor of being one of the coldest places in the world where people live year-round. Yet, the word “cold” barely describes it. Try “freeze your eyeballs frigid”. Oymyakon’s temperatures dip far below freezing. Its dwellers have to wear goggles to protect their eyes from very strong winds and severely cold temperatures. The land is mostly inhospitable to farming crops. So the locals eat mainly meat and canned foods brought in from other towns. 

![A snowy street with brightly colored buildings in the small town of Oymyakon, Russia.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6afbe8647f2a675b2a36b2131492d8f6.jpg)
*Picture 2*

###Extremely Dry###

4. Make sure to hug your umbrella the next time you see it. Some areas of Chile’s Atacama desert in South America haven’t seen rain in 400 years. People get water for crops and for drinking by “harvesting” clouds, instead of depending on precipitation. When the fog rolls in from the Pacific Ocean, they collect __moisture__ with plastic sheets that drip into pans underneath.

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5532f15ea7cd7f0de3cbe68c219a654e.jpg)
*Picture 3*

###Extremely Wet###

5. Your picnic got rained out? Absorb this: in Mawsynram, India, it basically *always* rains. The jungle village gets enough yearly rain to cover a three-story building. To cope with the steady rainfall, villagers have come up with some clever solutions. They use grass to soundproof their roofs from the constant noise of heavy rain. Outdoor workers rely on traditional *knups* to keep them dry. Woven with bamboo, grasses, and banana leaves, and worn over the head and back, these coverings look like turtle shells. 

6. No matter what your personal weather peeve is, there’s always a way to make the best of it. As the saying goes, “there’s no such thing as bad weather, only the wrong clothes.” People who live in the world's most intense environments realize that you’ve got to adapt to the climate you’ve got. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice Questions##

This question has two parts. Answer Part A, then Part B.

###Part A###

Read the sentence from paragraph 4.

“When the fog rolls in from the sea, they collect __moisture__ with plastic sheets that drip into pans underneath.”

**What is the meaning of the word __moisture__ in this sentence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

###Part B###

**Which of these words gives a clue to the meaning of __moisture__?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

*Psst! Don't forget to choose an answer for both questions* :)

options.definition: when a liquid turns to vapor or gas

6. No matter what your personal weather __peeve__ is, there’s always a way to make the best of it. As the saying goes, “there’s no such thing as bad weather, only the wrong clothes.” People who live in the world's most intense environments realize that you’ve got to adapt to the climate you’ve got. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice Question##

Read the sentence from paragraph 6.

“No matter what your personal weather __peeve__ is, there’s always a way to make the best of it.” 

Complete the sentence by choosing the correct answer from the dropdown menu.

**Based on the sentence, a __peeve__ is something that [[☃ dropdown 1]].**

Outdoor workers rely on traditional *knups* to keep them dry. Woven with bamboo, grasses, and banana leaves, and worn over the head and back, these coverings look like turtle shells. 

6. No matter what your personal weather peeve is, there’s always a way to make the best of it. As the saying goes, “there’s no such thing as bad weather, only the wrong clothes.” People who live in the world's most intense environments realize that you’ve got to adapt to the climate you’ve got. 

[[☃ explanation 1]]

___

##Practice Questions##

This question has two parts. Answer Part A, then Part B.

###Part A###

Read the excerpt from paragraph 4.

“Make sure to __hug your umbrella__ the next time you see it. Some areas of Chile’s Atacama desert in South America haven’t seen rain in 400 years.”

**Why do you think the author uses the phrase __hug your umbrella__?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

###Part B###

**What’s a word that could replace the word “hug” to more clearly show the same idea?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

*Psst! Don't forget to choose an answer for both questions* :)

options.definition: when a liquid turns to vapor or gas

options.explanation:
Estimated 900L

choices.content: to show that you shouldn’t complain about rain when some places barely get rain at all
to say that umbrellas are very expensive in parts of the world
to compare modern rain gear to India’s traditional *knups*
to explain that you should take care of your things so that you have them when they’re needed
